options.text: Mount Everest is the highest mountain in the world.
options.because: 'Highest' is the correct superlative of 'high' (one syllable: high → higher → highest). Superlatives use 'the' + adjective + -est.

options.text: Mount Everest is the most high mountain in the world.
options.because: One-syllable adjectives form superlatives with '-est', not 'most': 'the highest', not 'the most high'.

options.text: She is more taller than her brother.
options.because: Never use 'more' AND '-er' together. For short adjectives: taller (not 'more taller'). 'More' is used for long adjectives (more interesting).

options.text: This is the goodest cake I've ever tasted.
options.because: 'Good' has an irregular comparative and superlative: good → better → best. Not 'gooder' or 'goodest'.

explanation: Comparatives and superlatives: 1-syllable adjectives → -er/-est (tall/taller/tallest); 2+ syllables → more/most (beautiful/more beautiful/most beautiful); irregular: good/better/best, bad/worse/worst.

prompt: Complete with the correct comparative form.
template: A car is {{blank_1}} (expensive) than a bicycle.
explanation: 'Expensive' has three syllables, so it forms its comparative with 'more': 'more expensive'. One-syllable adjectives use -er: tall → taller. Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y use -ier: happy → happier.

prompt: 'As...as' is used to show that two things are equal in a particular quality.
becauseTrue: Correct. 'As + adjective + as' expresses equality: 'She is as tall as her sister.' The negative form 'not as...as' or 'not so...as' expresses inequality: 'He is not as fast as she is.'
becauseFalse: Incorrect. 'As...as' does express equality: 'This book is as interesting as that one.' The negative 'not as...as' shows that two things are different: 'He isn't as old as he looks.'
